items.q: What is the difference between market pull and technology push?
items.correct: Market pull means consumer demand drives development of a product; technology push means a new technology or material inspires a product the market hasn't asked for yet.
items.misconception: Market pull means pulling unsuccessful products off the shelves.
items.random_wrong: Technology push is advertising gadgets on social media.
items.why: Students guess from the everyday meanings of the words; the pair describes two opposite starting points for innovation and is a classic definition question.

items.q: Is a brand just a logo?
items.correct: No — a brand is the whole identity: name, logo, design language, values and reputation, shaping what customers expect from every product the company makes.
items.misconception: A brand is only the logo printed on the product.
items.random_wrong: A brand is the barcode used at the checkout.
items.why: Students reduce branding to graphics; understanding brand as accumulated reputation explains why companies defend it so fiercely.

items.q: Does successful crowdfunding guarantee a product will reach the market?
items.correct: No — crowdfunding provides finance and evidence of demand, but backers carry risk: funded projects can still fail in manufacture, certification or distribution.
items.misconception: Once a product is crowdfunded it is certain to be manufactured and delivered.
items.random_wrong: Crowdfunding is a government scheme that insures new products.
items.why: Students treat funding as the final hurdle, overlooking the engineering and logistical risks between prototype and delivered product.

items.q: What are the 4 Ps of the marketing mix?
items.why: Correct answer: Product, price, place, promotion. “Plan, prototype, produce, profit” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.correct: Product development driven by consumer demand
items.misconception: Products created because a new technology became available
items.why: Correct answer: Product development driven by consumer demand. “Products created because a new technology became available” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.correct: Raising finance from many small contributors via an online platform
items.misconception: A government grant for new factories
items.random_wrong: A bank loan secured against machinery
items.why: It's Raising finance from many small contributors via an online platform. “A government grant for new factories” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What is the main purpose of branding?
items.correct: To build recognition, trust and customer loyalty
items.misconception: To reduce the cost of raw materials
items.why: The answer examiners want is: To build recognition, trust and customer loyalty. “To reduce the cost of raw materials” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A product costs 12 pounds to manufacture and sells for 30 pounds. What is the profit per unit?
items.why: Exact answer: 18 £. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
